back_translation: When they had traveled through the city of Amphipolis and the city of Apollonia, they came to the city of Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Jews.
According to what Paul customarily practiced, he went in to them, and on three Sabbath days he reasoned and disputed with them from the Scriptures.
By explaining and proving that the Christ had to suffer and rise from the dead, and saying, 'This Jesus whom I am proclaiming to you is the Christ.'
Some of them believed and joined Paul and Silas, together with a great number of God-fearing Greeks, and not a few prominent women.

But the Jewish leaders became jealous, so they took some wicked men from the marketplace, formed themselves into a disturbing crowd, and incited the city to riot. They raided Jason's house, hoping to bring Paul and Silas out to deliver them to the crowd.

When they did not find Paul and Silas, they dragged Jason and some brothers to the city governing officials, shouting, 'These men who have stirred up trouble throughout the world have now come here also,'
'whom Jason has welcomed into his home. And all these people act contrary to the imperial decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, namely Jesus.'
Such words made the crowd and the city governing officials disturbed in mind.
When they had received bail money from Jason and the others, they released them.
The brothers immediately sent Paul and Silas off to the city of Berea by night. When the two arrived, they entered the synagogue of the Jews.

These Bereans had a more noble spirit than the Thessalonians; they received the word with eager enthusiasm, and searched the Scriptures every day to see whether these things were so according to that.
Therefore many of them believed, including upper-class Greek women and not a few men.
But when the Jewish leaders from the city of Thessalonica learned that Paul was also proclaiming the word of God in the city of Berea, they went there to incite and agitate the crowds.
The brothers immediately sent Paul out to the seacoast, but Silas and Timothy remained there.
Those who escorted Paul brought him as far as the city of Athens, and having received instructions for Silas and Timothy to come to Paul as quickly as possible, they then departed.
While Paul was waiting for Silas and Timothy in the city of Athens, his spirit was greatly troubled within him when he saw that throughout the city was full of idols.
So he reasoned and disputed in the synagogue with the Jews and the Gentiles who feared God, and in the marketplace every day with the people who passed by.

Some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers also came to debate with him. Some said, 'What is this seed-picker trying to say?' And others said, 'He seems to be a proclaimer of foreign gods,' because he was preaching the good news of Jesus and the resurrection from the dead.
So they brought him to the Areopagus, asking, 'May we know what this new teaching is that you are speaking?
For you are bringing some strange things for us to hear, so we want to know what these things mean.'
The Athenians and the foreigners who lived there spent their leisure on nothing more than telling or hearing the latest new things.
So Paul stood up in the midst of the council of the Areopagus and said, 'People of Athens, I see that you are especially strict in religion in every way.

For when I was walking through and examining the things you worship, I even found an altar with this inscription: "To the Unknown God." Therefore what you worship without knowing — this is what I proclaim to you.
The God who created the world and everything in it, this one is Lord of the heavens and the earth, does not dwell in temples that have been built by human hands;
nor is He served by human hands, as if He needed anything, since He Himself gives life, breath, and all things to all people;
and from one man, He created every nation of humanity to dwell throughout the earth, having determined the appointed times that He has set, and the boundaries of their dwelling places,
so that they might seek God, in the hope that they would grope for Him and find Him, even though He is not far from each one of us.
For 'in Him we live, move, and have our being,' as some of your own poets have also said: 'For we too are His offspring.'
Therefore, since we are God's offspring, we should not think that God is like gold, silver, or stone — an image sculpted by human craftsmanship and imagination.
Therefore, although God overlooked the times of ignorance, now He commands all people everywhere to repent.
For He has appointed a day on which He will judge the world with righteousness, by a man whom He has appointed; and He has provided proof to all by raising that man from the dead.'
When they heard about the resurrection from the dead, some mocked, but others said, 'We will hear you about this matter another time.'
So Paul departed from their midst.
But there were some who joined him and believed; among them were Dionysius, a member of the Areopagus council, and a woman named Damaris, and others who were with them.
